event: Early hunter-gatherer peoples settle the region that becomes Paraguay, leaving stone tools and rock art at sites such as Cerro Corá.
Guaraní-speaking peoples migrate into the area and develop semi-sedentary agriculture based on cassava, maize, and beans.
Tupí-Guaraní communities expand across the eastern forests while Guaycuruan and other peoples inhabit the Gran Chaco to the west.
Portuguese explorer Aleixo Garcia crosses the region with Guaraní allies, becoming the first European to traverse the area en route to the Inca realm.
Spanish conquistadors found Asunción, which becomes the principal Spanish base in southeastern South America.
Asunción is designated capital of the Spanish province, and explorer Álvar Núñez Cabeza de Vaca arrives as governor.
Jesuit missionaries begin work in the region to convert and organize Guaraní communities.
The Jesuits found their first major reductions, building a network of self-governing mission towns that flourish economically and culturally.
The Spanish crown splits the territory, separating Paraguay from the Río de la Plata province centered on Buenos Aires.
The Comuneros Revolt erupts in Asunción, an early colonial uprising against royal and Jesuit authority lasting until 1735.
King Charles III expels the Jesuits from Spanish America, leading to the decline of the Guaraní mission system.
Paraguay is incorporated into the newly created Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata.
Paraguay declares independence from Spain in May, also rejecting subordination to Buenos Aires.
José Gaspar Rodríguez de Francia is named Supreme Dictator, beginning an isolationist regime that consolidates the nation.
Dictator Francia dies after decades of rule that kept Paraguay independent but largely closed off from the outside world.
Carlos Antonio López becomes president and begins modernizing Paraguay with railroads, foundries, and foreign trade.
Francisco Solano López succeeds his father as president and pursues an ambitious, militarized foreign policy.
The War of the Triple Alliance begins as Paraguay fights Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay.
The catastrophic War of the Triple Alliance ends with the death of Solano López, after the conflict kills a majority of Paraguay's male population.
Paraguay adopts a new constitution under Allied occupation and cedes large territories to Brazil and Argentina.
The Colorado and Liberal parties are founded, establishing the rival forces that dominate Paraguayan politics for over a century.
A Liberal revolution overthrows the Colorado government, beginning decades of Liberal-dominated but unstable rule.
The Chaco War breaks out between Paraguay and Bolivia over the disputed Gran Chaco region.
The Chaco War ends with a ceasefire that leaves Paraguay in control of most of the contested Chaco.
A peace treaty formally awards Paraguay roughly three-quarters of the Gran Chaco.
Paraguay declares war on the Axis powers and becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
A brutal civil war devastates the country, strengthening the Colorado Party's grip on power.
General Alfredo Stroessner seizes power in a coup, beginning a 35-year authoritarian dictatorship.
A new constitution entrenches Stroessner's rule while maintaining a facade of democratic institutions.
Paraguay and Brazil sign the Itaipú Treaty to build one of the world's largest hydroelectric dams on the Paraná River.
The binational Itaipú Dam begins generating electricity, transforming Paraguay's energy economy.
Stroessner is overthrown in a coup led by General Andrés Rodríguez, ending the dictatorship.
Paraguay joins Argentina, Brazil, and Uruguay in founding the Mercosur trade bloc.
A new democratic constitution is enacted, guaranteeing civil liberties and recognizing Guaraní as an official language alongside Spanish.
Juan Carlos Wasmosy becomes the first civilian elected president in decades.
An attempted coup by General Lino Oviedo is thwarted, testing the young democracy.
Vice President Luis María Argaña is assassinated, triggering the deadly Marzo Paraguayo crisis and President Cubas's resignation.
Former bishop Fernando Lugo wins the presidency, ending 61 years of unbroken Colorado Party rule.
President Lugo is removed through a swift impeachment after a deadly land-dispute clash at Curuguaty.
Horacio Cartes of the Colorado Party is elected president, returning the party to power.
Protests over a constitutional reelection proposal lead to the burning of part of the Congress building in Asunción.
Santiago Peña of the Colorado Party wins the presidency, continuing the party's long political dominance.
